# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What are some of the best end of the world movies?

**Score:** 53
**Body:** I'm looking for apocalyptic, end of the world, judgment day, human extinction kind of movies. I never watch movies, so even the most obvious movies you guys can think of, I probably have never watched or know of it. Any suggestions?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Seeking a Friend for the End of the World (2012)
2. Children of Men
3. Deep Impact is fun. 2012. How It Ends.
4. Miracle Mile
5. Twelve Monkeys

The World's End

A Boy And His Dog

Children of Men

Sunshine

Snowpiercer

Cabin in the Woods
6. Melancholia
7. The Stand original series. M, o, o, n. That spells apocalypse.
8. Knowing with Nicholas cage is pretty good I haven’t seen it in years though
9. Don’t Look Up is funny and true and interesting on a bunch of levels.
10. On the beach

## 2. War movies to watch?

**Score:** 51
**Body:** Hello, guys, I’m in the mood for a war movie tonight. Any recommendations? Old or new, any country is fine, too. Just looking for something really worth watching. Thank youuuu! 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Platoon, black hawk down, saving private Ryan, apocalypse now, full metal jacket
2. Das Boot
3. The Bridge on the River Kwai (1957)  
The Hurt Locker (2008)   
The Killing Fields (1984)  
Das Boot (1981)  
Glory (1989)  
Letters From Iwo Jima (2006)  
1917 (2019)  
The Last of the Mohicans (1992)  
Three Kings (1999)  
Wings (1927)
  - Glory is a great recommendation
4. 1917, All quiet on the western front, journeys end, the trench, birdsong, darkest hour
5. The thin red line

Good morning Vietnam

Fury

All quiet on the Western front
6. Kelly’s Heroes!
7. Saving Private Ryan

Lawrence of Arabia
  - Lawrence of Arabia... director's cut!
8. Stalag 17

## 3. old british movies to watch on a rainy day?

**Score:** 46
**Body:** a bit vague, but the weather is making me want to watch a movie. preferably a **movie from the 20th century**, and if possible, a **british film**. any genre will do! the first thought that came to mind was withnail and i but i don't feel like rewatching it yet. i want something i haven't seen before LOL thanks in advance

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. *”Brassed Off”* (1996) is a very touching film based around the Grimethorpe Colliery Brass Band in a Coal Mining town that is facing closure during Margaret Thatcher’s tenure as Prime Minister, when coal mines were being closed across the country, devastating the small communities and towns that had grown around them.

It is a bittersweet story based on a real Band in a real town that shows the real world effects of large scale technological and economic change, through a very simple, personal and powerful lens.

Lots of great music, drama, tears and ultimately, a heartfelt and poignant ending. Well worth watching, even for those who don’t have an understanding of the political events and history of the time.
2. The Long Good Friday
3. A Matter Of Life And Death

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/A_Matter_of_Life_and_Death_%281946_film%29?wprov=sfla1
4. Ealing comedies are always good eg. The Ladykillers or Kind Hearts and Coronets. (I haven’t seen The Lavender Hill mon, but I assume it’s great). 

Looking for Eric, not without some grimness, but in the end a surprisingly feel-good movie, given it’s by Ken Loach
5. Kind Hearts and Coronets
6. The 39 steps
7. Murder at the Gallop (1963) and the others in Margaret Rutherford’s Marple series

School for Scoundrels (1960)
8. Bedazzled (1969) with Peter Cook, Dudley Moore, Raquel Welch
9. Find a Merchant and Ivory film…Howards End and The Remains of the Day are great.
10. Whiskey Galore from 1949

## 4. Movies That 'Star' LA

**Score:** 45
**Body:** I'll be traveling to LA in about 5 weeks, until then I'd like to watch movies made BEFORE 1990 that can be considered to have Los Angeles as a "main character".

Here is a list of movies I've already seen that would evoke what I'm looking for:

Model Shop.  
Chinatown.  
Sunset Boulevard.   
Shampoo.  
Repo Man.  
❌ Mulholland Drive.  
The Long Goodbye.  
Fast Times at Ridgemont High.  
Killer of Sheep.  
Less Than Zero.  
To Live & Die in LA.  
The Decline of Western Civilization.  

Any genre is cool, the less obscure, low budget, underground the better. 

EDIT TO ADD:
LMAO , if there was ever post to point to as evidence that people only read the title, it could be this one!

People: *BEFORE* 1990.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's 1991 but you should absolutely include LA Story. 
2. Who Framed Roger Rabbit (1988)
3. Swingers. LA Confidential.
4. LA Story
5. Boyz n the Hood

It's from 1991, and it might be a different side of L.A. than you're looking for, but it's a great movie.
6. The Player (1992) technically doesn’t match your criteria, however, it’s close enough to be worth a watch in my opinion.
7. Dragnet. Starring Tom Hanks and Dan Ackrpyd. Not a great movie but it visits several iconic sites that were around in the 80s. Santa Monica Pier, Griffith Observatory, The Brown Derby, The old Police HQ. Muscle Beach. Like all over actual places that are/were in and around Los Angeles.
8. Check out the film documentary Los Angeles Plays Itself (2003). 🙂
9. Do movies that take place before 1990 count?
The Nice Guys and Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
10. Miracle Mile (1988)

## 5. Going to take an edible and looking for movie recommendations

**Score:** 36
**Body:** As the title says, my GF and I are going to stay in tonight and looking for a good movie to watch after taking an edible.

We want to blow our minds like Interstellar or Arrival but obviously not those movies. But same general vibe would be perfect. 

Any suggestions? Thanks in advance! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Annihilation
  - Ended up going with this one. Wow. Was exactly what we were looking for.
2. Holy Mountain
3. Koyaanisqatsi
4. Annihilation -or- The Fountain

They Came Together is also a fantastic film to watch with your partner stoned
5. Bugonia with Emma Stone I think is her name, blew me away.
6. With edible? You need something grandiose: The Fall (2006) watch the trailer you won’t regret it!
7. I bet The Fifth Element would be fun while stoned lol
8. It's all visuals, but Baraka (1992) is fantastic
9. A Color Out of Space or Mandy if you want nightmarish psychedelic visual spectacles with a side of Nick Cage fueled chaos

Seven Psychopaths if you want to giggle at the absurdity of Colin Farrell and Sam Rockwell 

The Boy And The Heron (or Princess Mononoke, or Spirited Away) if you want some delicious classic anime visuals with compelling storylines

Apocalypse Now if you want one of the only war movies that actually benefits from a stoned viewing (Full Metal Jacket kinda scratches the itch but nothing beats Apocalypse Now)

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Thoughts on Sentimental Value

**Score:** 42
**Body:** I just saw Sentimental Value in theaters and was totally blown away. It's the first Trier film I've seen, and its poetic, meditative nature is stunningly beautiful. It's one of those movies where I sat and watched it all the way through the end credits, and just sat outside in the night air reflecting on the emotions of the film. Such a life affirming film and a film director's confessional in the way that Vertigo or After Life (Koreeda) reflect their respective director's contemplation on filmmaking itself.

Some thoughts:

\- At the core of the film is a reflection on film itself - the beautiful power of film to move audiences (through Rachel Kemp, the famous actress) and the act of creating a film itself as catharsis. But its also about the capacity of film making to be an escape from reality and responsibilities, as the daughter confronts the father about how he only made her the center of his universe when they were making a film yet he abandoned them afterwards. This paradoxical nature of art as capable of both escapism and a confrontation of reality really resonates with me.

\- The use of frequent cuts to black not only have aesthetic/poetic value between scenes as a caesura, but also highlights the nature of film itself - rather than creating an escapist illusion as most films do, this film reminds the audience of the medium itself. Its use in this film seems to emphasize the nature of film (as empty film stock with nothing exposed), in contrast to digital movies (see the point below). It reminds me of impasto in art, in which the physical nature of the medium is embraced, rather than trying to maintain an illusion by covering it up.

\- In the same way as the film is about Sentimental Value (the characters reconciling their complicated relationship with their own pasts, the long history of the house and the family tree), the film is also a reflection on how film itself is a dying art in the age of Netflix, streaming. For one, such a poetic, slow film as Sentimental Value is losing popularity and an audience in 2025. The film uses Netflix studio demands, the uncertainty of films being presented in theaters, the aging and frailty of the old masters to represent a sense of ephemeral - the father's final film is his last attempt to revive the past, both his family past and the old ways of cinema.

\-One insane detail of the film is how when Rachel Kemp recited the dialogue on prayer, it really felt like the actress within the film was pouring her heart out, yet something felt a little hollow and empty. Yet when Nora later more casually reads the dialogue, something about it instantly clicks, like the dialogue had finally found the home it was intended for, even though Nora isn't trying to perform it. Of course this was the intention within the film, but I am just in such awe of the performances of Elle Fanning and Renate Reinsve to pull off this effect. Especially Elle Fanning - acting as an actress trying to perform this dialogue that she is trying to wrap her her head around, desperate to please this legendary filmmaker yet subconsciously aware that the role is not meant for her. And goddamn, what a stunningly powerful piece of dialogue. The scene where Nora recites the dialogue and then embraces her baby sister moved me to tears in a way that other films have not done in a very long time.

On the criticism that the happy ending was unearned:

I've read on some other posts that they felt the ending was a neat little bow that was undeserved because the father had not earned forgiveness, a cliche that ruined the previous nuance and subtleties. I couldn't disagree more.

\- I understand that it is currently in vogue to cut off one's family the second they become any sort of burden (and I don't want to minimize the very real situations of abusive families where this is the right choice), but this is a very old-fashioned story that portrays the bonds between family as undeniable and inescapable - which is precisely the reason why family relationships hurt us the most, yet makes the catharsis of rediscovering and reaffirming those bonds so powerful. Also, the dad was 'wrong' to abandon the daughters, but he wasn't abusive or anything extreme and outside of reason like that. Its clear that the desire to have a relationship with the father eats away at the daughters - they are still fundamentally the little girls wanting to spend time with their daddy. There is this constant yearning for each other that is undeniable, a yearning that has been mutated and rotted and battered by all the years, yet it is still there.

\-The dad is presented as part of a larger picture of generational trauma, as he was 7 when his own mother committed suicide. Can we judge him so harshly for leaving the house where his mother committed suicide, where the remnants of his broken heart and shattered marriage lay? He is as human as anyone - in the same way that Nora is avoidant of her problems, as shown through dating a married man, her stage fright, her missing calls from her sister and later calling in sick when all of her repressed emotions start rotting within her, the father is avoidant of his traumas and feelings, only able to engage with reality through filmmaking (or alcohol). He says the best thing that happened to him is his two daughters, and his love for them is palpable - this whole film is about a broken man, a lost man trying to reclaim his relationship with his daughters in the only way he knows how, through film. The fact that the script he writes so accurately portrays Nora's past depression as if he was there - he sees himself in her, he cares so deeply about her and understand her, but there is just so much lost time and hurt feelings that have gotten in the way. To be so cut and dry, so calculated about how he hasn't sufficiently earned forgiveness for his wrongdoings is just not true to life for me - every one is fucked up, everyone has their internal demons and irrationalities that causes them to act in ways opposed to their wants and needs, yet for the most part we need to try to come together and find some solace and connection despite the insanity of it all. Sometimes we seek connections with people who have hurt us even if it seems they don't deserve it, sometimes we overreact and destroy connections even though we don't really want to. Such is life and being human.

\-The "forgiveness" takes place through reading the script and Agnes' research into her grandmother's torture, understanding her father's trauma. We don't get to see the full script, we just see that one quote that Nora recites, which is implied to reflect the father's yearning to be with his daughters. Ultimately, the relationship between the script and the sisters having that one moment of remembering their bonds with each other is so intimate, so personal that it cannot be revealed explicitly on the screen - so it may come off as unearned to the viewer (see point on negative space below). And that the final scene represents full on forgiveness is also dubious (see below).

\- The idea of family itself is symbolized through the character of the house, which has held that family for generations. The house in the beginning of the family is old and worn down - structurally broken and slowly sinking, like the trauma in the family that started with the grandmother's torture and suicide that has caused generational trauma. Appropriately, Nora self identifies with the house as a child - her period of connection and yearning for family, yet she feels her grade school assignment on this idea is shallow in her young adulthood when auditioning as an actress, symbolizing her numbness and avoidance of family issues. By the end of the film, the house is recreated, given new life through filmmaking - Trier takes time to present these beautiful shots of the refurbished house. Yet like family, the house is something that cannot be denied, cannot be torn down casually, no matter how battered and worn down it gets.

\-The ending is not some cliched happy ending - there are plenty of ambiguities within the ending and lingering questions. We don't know what their relationship will be like after the film, we don't know in the hearts of each characters how much they have forgiven each other or how much they still struggle with the demons of the past. All that we know is that through the act of filmmaking, in that moment alone, there is finally a sense of restored daughter-father relationships, even if imperfect. In some sense its fucked up that the dad needs his daughter to be his actress in order to rekindle that sense of connection, but it's the only way he knows how to - I think this is also Trier's confessional statement on the power of filmmaking and his own experiences with directing as well, that intimacy and connection between director and actor.

\-This also speaks to one of the best features of this film - it leaves so much unsaid, it has the perfect amount of negative space (as represented by the cuts to black). There are so many plot events that are implied to have happened yet are not shown on screen (such as the old cinematographer showing up at the final scene, despite it seeming before that the father wasn't sure whether he could use him), and each scene is blatantly shown to be from a different character's perspective - we never get the full picture. My favorite example is the decision not to show Nora talking about her acceptance of the role with her father - we don't know whether this was a teary, heartfelt affair, or whether it was a curt, business-like discussion, or whether Nora makes clear that this doesn't make up for all of the hurt in the past. We just know that the family is willing to make this one step forward together, and that's all that matters.

One question I have is about who is talking during the voice overs. In the opening, I think its the father talking about Nora's school assignments, but there are other voice overs where an older woman is talking. I'm curious who people think this is. Maybe the ambiguity is the point - the perspective we are viewing the film from is left unclear.

TL;DR - perfect movie. Please reconsider if you think the ending was cliched and unearned. Sorry for all the typos and grammatical errors.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I thought it was the best of the year. Im a PTA fanboy but this was the only film that really exceeded my expectations. I would say its Triers best and that saying a lot coming off Worst Person.
  - Me too. I liked One Battle… and it’s very good filmmaking but Sentimental Value to me had so much more depth. 

Triers seems to be able to get such nuanced performances from his actors. Some of the small things Stellan Skarsgård was doing for his character were so special and gave authenticity to his role.

I think Sentimental Value will probably age very well and in two decades and beyond when there is a retrospective the film will be considered the best released in 2025.
2. I’ve read that there’s an adaptation of Don DeLillo’s novel Zero K that is going to be produced. That book looks at tensions between a father and his son. The father is a billionaire and his son above all resents the shadow that such a prominent figure casts over his life. The father, additionally, is a difficult personality, self-absorbed and workaholic.

I see some of the comments here saying of the Skarsgard character, was he abusive, and so on? I’m sure you’re not meant to think that he did anything worse than departing to another country when he divorced the two girls’ mother when they were still young, and progressing with his career without anymore taking the girls into the fold, into the field of view of the things that really concerned him in life, which was his work. I’m not sure about the film of which an excerpt is shown: does he cast his daughter in this just before the divorce, or a few years after it? In either case, they have, in the making of that film, a concrete instance of what it feels like to occupy the full focus of their father’s attention, and to understand what he’s depriving them of, over and above his absence in their home. It’s clear that he lives for his work, which is also the basis of his identity; that his work, which is himself, totally eclipses his family cannot be otherwise, because he is selfish on a grand scale. That’s all that’s necessary for his daughters to feel how they do about him.

Through the lens of Gustav at work he seems like a decent person. It might be conventional to think of events like the Venice Film Festival shown in the film as somehow tainted by money and exclusivity, etc., but it really is also a venue in which extraordinary artists, whom we don’t think of as vulnerable because of their fame, power and wealth, show up and feel extremely vulnerable, and they show off their new work, and hope that their peers are kind to it. Gustav and Rachel Kemp make this connection there. Throughout, when he is directing her in rehearsal, he seems like someone who it is a positive experience to work with and be around.

We see in the end that he’s using his regular, now limping cinematographer. Earlier he was very quick to agree to the practical decision to dump him. Doesn’t seem very nice. No explanation is given as to how he has him back in the end, it feels more like a joke. (I believe also, though, that the film is no longer meant to be a Netflix production.) When Gustav first drives in his Volvo SUV to the home of the cinematographer, there’s a pop song playing. It reminds me of the opening of the book The End in the My Struggle series, where the writer describes driving out to meet an old friend and colleague, and to lay out the complex history between them — or maybe the detailed circumstances of their present lives — and adds that he’s listening to Cat People by David Bowie on his car’s speakers. 

I wonder if, for a Norwegian audience, the situation in the film is intended to evoke Knausgaard at all (a writer of whom it’s said more than 10% of everyone in the whole country read his book A Death in the Family)? To go back to the point I raised about Zero K and its familial generational conflict: the Gustav character would need hardly to have done more than have been a national figure, and conceivably his children could dislike him for it. Nevermind that maybe his art is sometimes revealing of painful private realities. Nevermind that he also left his children behind following a divorce.

Beyond this discussion of the Gustav character, I’ll respond to two points in the OP. The fades to black: I heard about this before seeing the film. I expected it to be a more prominent aspect than it really is. I don’t know if there’s anything to take away from this feature, personally.

Also whether it was more genuine when the daughters were looking at the scene about prayer, than when it was being played by the Rachel Kemp character. I don’t know. I wrote a thread on this sub a few days ago, which briefly touches on Sentimental Value, and approved of the dramatic “risks” it consistently takes as it progresses in order to develop and complexify its story.

https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/comments/1q6ljw5/dramatic_understetedness_recessiveness_and/

I don’t specifically refer to it, but one of those risks I’m talking about is specifically the revisiting of this scene at a point which, once the film has concluded, one can look back to and understand it to be the dramatic climax of the film. Does the writing of the scene from the film within the film “come off” well enough that it can not only sustain being revisited like this, but is this whole scene (the diegetic scene of the two sisters looking at the script in the actress’s apartment) effective at achieving what you say it does: at proving that the daughter is the natural to play the part, etc., etc.? I don’t know if I can say that I totally felt this on my first viewing of the film.
3. I loved *almost* everything about this movie. I loved the quiet and contemplative storytelling, the performances, the voice overs, the exploration of generational trauma... the director's  trust in the audience (for the vast majority of the film).

My one complaint was the scene with the 3 generations of faces morphing together repeatedly near the end. It was such an on the nose cheeseball heavy handed touch from a director who otherwise has done a fantastic job of trusting his audience to hang with the (certainly not overly subtle) themes of the film. It felt like all of a sudden he was yelling IN CASE YOU MISSED IT HERE IS THE THEME OF THE MOVIE!!! DON'T MISS IT!!!!!! In an 'I am 14 and this is deep' kind of way. 

Still gave it 5 stars, but that scene keeps nagging at me.

Btw, if you haven't seen 'The Worst Person In The World' it is incredible.
  - Yeah I have to agree with you on that one. Maybe its a specific homage to another film? Reminded me of vertigo a bit.  



Although was it the 3 generations? I thought it was the dad and the 2 daughters, didn't see any old lady mixed in there.
4. Thanks for sharing your perspective and reflections on how the film resonated with you. I am someone who felt like the father’s forgiveness felt unearned, but I appreciated reading your arguments against that attitude.

You write: “Its clear that the desire to have a relationship with the father eats away at the daughters” but this did not come through to me at all. I found that they spent the bulk of the movie wanting nothing to do with him and repeatedly saying how difficult he is to be around. That’s why it felt unearned, but maybe I didn’t pick up on the subtext of yearning that you did.

I echo your appreciation of the “negative space” and all that was left unsaid. I liked how the story unfolded in suggestion and rough outlines. You make a good point that we aren’t shown how Nora approached her father after reading the script, so you’re right that it may not be as straightforward a forgiveness as some might make it out to be. That said, because of the negative space, we don’t see any part of how Gustav treated his daughters as they were growing up. We know he was absent and an alcoholic. He comes across somewhat brusque and rude. Was he physically or emotionally abusive? Was he unsupportive? Was he authoritarian and controlling? We don’t see this, so perhaps the disconnect between your reaction to the film and others who felt he was forgiven too easily comes from each viewer having a more or less charitable interpretation of Gustav as a father.

Anyway, thank you for explaining what you loved so much about the film. I enjoyed many parts of it, even though I have mixed feelings about the resolution. For me, it felt simultaneously too facile but also exactly how this man would try to reconcile with his daughter. 
  - Thanks for your honest and thoughtful response. I guess any such film with so much negative space lends itself to a lot of input from the eyes of the audience, so the different subtexts will vary significantly from person to person. You make a good point that their past is also obscured - we know the dad is an alcoholic, and as many male artists can be, he is very difficult and blunt. The extent of their hurt is also unknown to us, although abandoning one's daughters is already pretty severe. 



I guess I read the yearning in that cliched idea of "hate isn't the opposite of love but just another form of it" - Nora clearly has so many repressed emotions regarding her father that leeches into so many aspects of her, but this immense emotion seems that of a little girl who just wanted her father around and feels abandoned. Especially Nora seemed to really want her father to like theatre and her work, she cared about that validation from him. Their understanding that the father won't be around forever and understanding of his own trauma from his grandmother is important as well. I think its the power of that dialogue on prayer and the connection it provides between the daughter and father that was most convincing to me that the daughters would find it within themselves to at least try to take this step forward.
5. Full disclosure: I absolutely adored The Worst Person in the World. And this was one of my most anticipated of the year. Ended up being a tremendous disappointment.

Directorial wise, it was so much blander than Worst Person, which had a lyrical, poetic quality to its direction, while still feeling understated. This movie felt understated, but with none of the flair that Worst Person had.
  - Could you specify what you mean by directorial flair? 

  
Respect your take, for sure I've gotta go see Worst Person in the World!
  - It sounds like you just want a larger dose of flair and a smaller dose of Bergman, which you are perfectly within your rights to wish for. But a lot of film fans will probably disagree with this as Bergman-style family dramas and psychological explorations are highly regarded by many here. I personally love both films but thought Trier’s ability to expand his themes into the lives of other characters in Sentimental Value, to say nothing of the superb dynamics between the lead actors, make it a superior work to Worst Person. And again, I do love the latter, own it on Bluray, rewatch it occasionally, etc. OP expands quite well on a lot of the nuances and wonderful aspects of Sentimental Value and I think a rewatch is going to be even better than my initial viewing.

---
